Chapter 1 — Official Meeting

It was a regular day at Avinav Architects. As usual, the office was buzzing with activity—employees busy with their work, back-to-back meetings lined up for the day.

But something was off.

Neither Avinav nor Ehsen had reached the office yet. And that was strange. The entire staff noticed. These two weren’t just the company’s owners—they were known for their punctuality. No matter what, they were always on time, setting an example that everyone admired. They believed that being an owner didn’t mean being above the rules. For them, discipline and equality mattered.

Finally, around 10 AM, both Avinav and Ehsen walked into the office.

The moment they arrived, Ria—Avinav’s assistant—headed straight to his cabin to inform him about the important meeting scheduled with the England architects regarding their upcoming project.

When Ria entered the cabin, she found the two of them standing near the table, arguing like school kids.

Ehsen: (folding his arms)
"Abinav, mujhe blame karna mat shuru kar. Tu hi late aaya tha mujhe pick karne!"
("Abinav, don’t even try to blame me. You came late to pick me up!")

Abinav: (rolling his eyes)
"Oh please, tu ne toh last minute pe bola mujhe aane ko!"
("Oh please, you told me at the last minute to come get you!")

Ehsen:
"Main kya karta? Car achanak se kharab ho gayi."
("What else could I do? My car broke down all of a sudden.")

Abinav: (with a smirk)
"Aur ab woh bhi meri galti hai? You could’ve just taken a cab!"
("And that’s my fault now? You could’ve just taken a cab!")

Ria stood at the door, trying not to laugh. The way they were complaining and teasing each other made her smile.

Ria: (smiling)
"You two are unbelievable. Mujhe nahi pata tha bade log itne cute tareeke se late hone pe lad sakte hain!"
("You two are unbelievable. I didn’t know grown-ups could fight this cutely over being late!")

Both of them looked at her, then at each other—and burst out laughing.

Still smiling, Ria stepped fully into the room.

Ria:
"Bas karo ab drama. I just came to remind you—we have that meeting with the England architects in 30 minutes. It’s about the new project."
("Okay, enough of your drama now. I came to remind you—we have that meeting with the England architects in 30 minutes. It’s about the new project.")

Avinav’s expression shifted from playful to serious.

Avinav:
"Right. Thanks for the heads-up, Ria. Let’s get things ready."

Ehsen: (grinning)
"Chalo, fight hold pe rakhni padegi. But bhoolna mat—abhi baaki hai!"
("Guess we’ll have to put our fight on hold. But don’t think I’m done with you, bro!")

Ria: (laughing)
"Please! Ye sab meeting ke baad kar lena."
("Please! Save it for after the meeting.")
Conference Room – Avinav Architects Office, India


The screen at the far end of the meeting room lit up as the video call connected. Avinav and Ehsen sat ready at the conference table, their expressions focused.

The screen split into two frames. On the left appeared Mr. Jonathan Reed, the dignified CEO of the UK-based architecture firm. On the right, a composed young woman with a confident posture and bright eyes—Zoya Khanna, head of the Unity Cultural Experience Centre project.

Jonathan Reed: (smiling)
“Good morning from London. It’s a pleasure to finally connect. We’re thrilled to begin this collaboration.”

Avinav: (nodding professionally)
“Good morning. We’ve been looking forward to this meeting. There’s something truly special about this project—it's not just about design; it’s about identity.”

Ehsen: (adding lightly)
“And when tradition meets innovation, you can expect fireworks. The good kind.”

Jonathan: (gesturing towards Zoya)
“Well said. Let me introduce the person leading this from our side—Zoya Khanna. She’ll be flying to India next week and will be working with your team on the ground from next month, when the official project phase begins.”

Zoya: (smiling politely)
“Hello, both of you. It’s wonderful to meet you—even virtually for now. I’ve gone through your firm's past work, and I’m really excited about what we can build together.”

Avinav: (curious, leaning slightly forward)
“We’d love to hear your vision, Zoya. What’s your take on the Unity Cultural Experience Centre?”

Zoya shared her screen. A sleek presentation appeared—concept sketches, mood boards, material samples, and structural layouts filled the screen.

Zoya:
“Our design philosophy blends narrative with functionality. For this project, I’m proposing:

Traditional courtyards to draw in natural light and wind,

Locally sourced materials like sandstone and terracotta,

Climate-responsive architecture using solar roofing and passive cooling,

And an amphitheater design that faces the river—symbolizing openness, unity, and cultural flow.”

A beat of silence passed as Avinav studied the screen.

Avinav: (impressed)
“You’ve managed to strike the emotional tone we were hoping for—right at the concept level. That’s rare.”

Zoya: (smiling softly)
“My goal is to create something that not only looks beautiful, but feels meaningful. I’ve spent years on heritage projects in both the UK and India—this one feels personal.”

Jonathan Reed: (pleased)
“She’s being humble. Zoya’s led some of our firm’s most culturally sensitive and award-winning designs. You’ll see once she’s there in person.”

Ehsen: (grinning)
“I already like the way she thinks. She gets the balance we always try to maintain—cultural roots with modern techniques.”

Zoya: (with quiet confidence)
“And I’m looking forward to collaborating closely once I’m there. I've heard your team works with a lot of passion. I’m ready for it.”

Avinav: (with a slight smile of admiration)
“We don’t just work with passion—we build with it. It’ll be great having someone like you join us. This is just the beginning.”

Jonathan Reed: (checking his watch)
“Well, that covers everything for today. Zoya will keep in touch over the next few weeks as we prepare for the official kickoff.”

Zoya: (smiling)
“I’ll also be sending over detailed site analysis reports and some refinements to the concept sketches. Looking forward to seeing you all in person soon.”

Avinav: (nodding)
“Same here. We’ll begin preparing our side as well. Thank you both.”

Ehsen:
“This is going to be one exciting project. Talk soon!”

Jonathan:
“Take care, gentlemen.”

Zoya:
“Bye for now!”

The screen fades to black as the call ends.

Ehsen leaned back in his chair, letting out a low whistle.

Ehsen:
"Okay, yeh toh maine expect hi nahi kiya tha. She’s... sharp. Matlab seriously sharp."
("Okay, I really didn’t expect that. She’s... sharp. Like, seriously sharp.")

Avinav: (smiling slightly, arms folded)
"Sharp hi nahi, clear bhi hai. She knows exactly what she wants the space to say—aur kaise kehna hai."
("Not just sharp, she’s also clear. She knows exactly what she wants the space to say—and how to make it say it.")

Ehsen: (grinning)
"Baat aise kar rahi thi jaise poora structure pehle hi uske dimaag mein ready ho. That vision? I like it."
("She spoke like someone who had already built the whole structure in her mind. That vision? I like it.")

Avinav: (thoughtful, looking at the dark screen)
"Mujhe kya impress kiya pata hai? The way she respects both cultures. Woh bas ek UK idea India mein nahi laa rahi—she’s actually building a bridge between both. That’s rare."
("You know what impressed me? The way she respects both cultures. She’s not just bringing a UK idea to India—she’s actually building a bridge between both. That’s rare.")

Ehsen: (teasing)
"Waise... tu toh kuch zyada hi focused lag raha tha jab woh bol rahi thi."
("Well... you seemed a little too focused when she was talking.")

Avinav: (chuckling)
"Kya karu, yaar. Aise log sirf building nahi banate—they design experiences. And the way she carries herself... confident, but calm. She’s definitely going to be a big part of this success."
("What can I say, man. People like her don’t just build structures—they design experiences. And the way she carries herself... confident, but calm. She’s definitely going to be a big part of this success.")

Ehsen:
"Lagta hai kisi ko fan banne mein der nahi lagi."
("Looks like someone became a fan real quick.")

Avinav: (smiling knowingly)
"Main talent ka fan hoon. Aur usmein woh definitely hai."
("I’m a fan of talent. And she definitely has it.")They both fall silent for a moment—each quietly excited about the journey ahead.

----------------------------------------------------

Chapter - 2 Homecoming

Zoya’s Office, London – Minutes After the Video Call Ends

Jonathan knocked lightly on Zoya’s glass door and stepped in, a knowing look on his face.

Jonathan: (smirking)
“So... thoughts?”

Zoya: (closing her laptop, relaxed)
“They’re sharp. Structured. The kind of team that doesn’t waste a single line whether in design or in conversation.”

Jonathan:
“And Avinav?”

Zoya: (pausing, then with a slight smile)
“He’s... focused. Quietly commanding. Doesn’t need to say much to be heard.”

Jonathan: (chuckling)
“Sounds like someone made an impression.”

Zoya: (grinning slightly, leaning back)
“Professionally? Absolutely. He reads a room the way some people read blueprints layer by layer.”

Jonathan: (nodding, thoughtful)
“He built that firm from scratch, you know. Still believes in hands-on work. No ego.”

Zoya:
“That shows. There’s honesty in their vision. I’m excited, Jonathan. This project it’s not just a job. It feels... rooted.”

Jonathan: (pleased)
“That’s exactly why I wanted you on this.”

Zoya gave a small smile, her fingers absentmindedly tracing a pencil across her sketchpad, where the words Unity Cultural Experience Centre – India Collaboration were scribbled at the top.

                 ***********

After a week 

The soft hum of the airplane surrounded Zoya as she stared out of the small window, watching clouds drift like distant memories.

Seven years.
Seven full years since she last saw Mumbai not through a screen, but with her own eyes.

First, it was the four-year whirlwind of her Engineering and Architectural Design MEng. Then, three more years working on some of the most demanding and rewarding projects in the UK. There had been deadlines, site visits, design reviews—and somewhere in between, life had quietly moved on without her.

Including back home.

She hadn’t even made it to her brother’s wedding.

Flashback – 3 years ago, a call from home

Aaryan (her brother): “Zoya, we waited for you to say yes. The date’s final. Are you coming?”

Zoya (heart tight, voice low): “Bhai… I wish I could. This restoration project—it’s an international feature. I’m presenting at the Royal Society next month. I...”

Ruhi (softly): “We understand, Zo. Just… don’t forget where home is.”

Zoya: “Never.”

She had whispered that word with conviction. But now, as the city lights of Mumbai twinkled below like fireflies, it hit her: how much she had missed.

The smell of monsoon-soaked earth. The crowded warmth of Marine Drive. Even the chaos of airport exits filled with families holding handmade placards.

She hadn’t just left a country. She had left a heartbeat behind.

Zoya blinked rapidly, willing away the sting in her eyes.

Her lips curved into a smile. For the first time in years, she wasn’t returning for a vacation or a quick visit. She was coming home to work, to create, to belong again.

And maybe just maybe to find something more.

As the wheels touched the runway, Zoya gripped the armrest, exhaled deeply, and whispered:

Zoya:
“Hello again, Mumbai. Let’s see what you’ve got for me this time.”

The arrival gate buzzed with the usual crowd, but for Zoya, everything felt surreal—her heart pounding like a drum in her chest as she scanned the sea of faces.

Then she saw him Aaryan standing there, eyes wide and searching, a nervous smile tugging at his lips.

The moment their eyes locked, his face broke into a radiant smile, and without a word, he pulled her into a fierce, protective hug.

Aaryan: (voice thick with emotion)
“Zoya… it’s really you. Seven years... way too long, little sister.”

Zoya’s breath hitched, tears slipping down her cheeks as she clung to him.

Zoya: (barely above a whisper)
“I missed you so much, Bhai. It’s been so hard without you.”
Suddenly, a bubbly voice chimed in behind her.

Ruhi:
“Zoya?! Oh my God, it’s really you!”

Zoya turned and saw Ruhi stepping forward with a huge, bright smile that lit up the entire space. Her eyes sparkled with joy, her arms wide open like she was welcoming a long-lost sister.

Zoya: (tears of happiness flowing)
“Bhabhi! I can’t believe this… finally meeting you in person after all these calls!”

Ruhi’s laughter rang out warm and genuine as she pulled Zoya into a tight, affectionate hug.

Ruhi: (laughing)
“I’ve waited for this moment forever! You’ve been like family to me already, but holding you now... it’s something else.”

Zoya felt the warmth and love pouring from Ruhi’s embrace, the kind of bond that needed no words—only heartbeats syncing in joy.

Ruhi: (pulling back, grinning)
“Now, no more screens or calls—we’re officially real-life sisters from today!”

Zoya smiled through her tears, feeling an overwhelming gratitude for this moment, this family, this homecoming.

When they left airport their car merged into traffic, Zoya gazed out the window.

Zoya:
“Wow… Mumbai looks so different. So many new buildings, the skyline has changed completely.”

Ruhi:
“Yeah, it’s grown fast. But some things remain the same the chaos, the energy.”

Aaryan: (smiling)
“And the traffic jams that never end.”

Zoya laughed softly, the familiar sounds and sights stirring memories.

They reached their home after a long journey all thanks to Mumbai traffic. The moment she seen her house floods of memories flown in front of her in a second. The door creaked open, and Zoya stepped inside her childhood home a flood of nostalgia rushing in.

Zoya: “This place… it hasn’t changed much. The streets, the corners… it still feels like home.”

Aaryan: (smiling warmly)
“Some things don’t need to change. Like the way this city holds you, no matter how far you go.”

Zoya walked slowly to her old room, fingertips grazing the faded paint on the walls, memories flooding back.

Zoya: (whispering)
“I still remember the day I left for England. Standing here, it felt like I was leaving a part of myself behind.”

Ruhi: (smiling warmly)
“You know, even though you were miles away, it always felt like you were right here with us—through every call, every message. You never really left.”

Aaryan: “We’ve been so proud of everything you’ve done, Zoya. But honestly… it just wasn’t the same without you around.”

Zoya looked at them both, her eyes shimmering with tears. In that moment, surrounded by their love, she felt the years of distance melt away, and the true meaning of home settle deep in her heart.


----------------------------------------------------

Chapter - 3 The Proposal: Hints of a New Beginning

Avinav Architects Studio – 7:30 PM

The sky outside the glass-paneled office had faded into dusky hues. Inside, the once-busy studio now echoed with silence, save for the soft tapping of keyboards and the occasional rustle of rolled blueprints.

Most of the team had left for the day, but Ehsen and Avinav remained—one scanning through final design mockups, the other reviewing tomorrow’s walkthrough schedule.

Ehsen leaned back in his chair, rubbing his eyes before glancing over at his friend.

Ehsen: (casual)
“Long day. You heading home soon?”

Avinav: (without looking up)
“Yeah. Just wrapping this last segment. Need to be sharp tomorrow.”

Ehsen:
“How’s Uncle and Aunty? And Pranav? Haven’t seen them in a while.”

Avinav:
“They’re doing well. You should visit home sometime—been a while since you came by. Even Mom and Dad asked about you.”

Ehsen:
"Soon"

Avinav:
“Alright. I’m off before Ma sends a rescue party. You good here?”

Ehsen:
“All set. Go home before they replace you with a better son.”

They both laughed, and with a final nod, Avinav left the office.

                                *******************
Avinav’s Home

The doors opened to the gentle glow of home soft light spilling out from beneath the door, the distant sound of a video game and his mother humming in the kitchen.

The moment he stepped in...

Pranav: (yelling from the couch, without looking up)
“Bhai! You’re officially two hours late. What’s the excuse this time? Alien abduction?”

Avinav:
“Naah… bas usual deadlines. Aur ek dost jo kabhi time pe office chhodta hi nahi.”
(Nope… just usual deadlines. And a friend who never leaves the office on time.)

Kavitha:
“Dinner bas ready hi hai. You go get freshen up, Mr Architect.”
(Dinner is almost ready. You go get freshen up, Mr Architect.)

Pranav:
“Bhai, agar kabhi cultural centres design karte karte bore ho jao, toh meri game pe kaam kar lena. Background score chahiye.”
(Bro, if you ever get bored designing cultural centers, help me with my game. I need a background score.)

Avinav:
“Zaroor. Bas mujhe Executive Producer ka credit milna chahiye yaani bada bhai jo tera Wi-Fi bill bharta hai.”
(Sure. As long as I get credit as Executive Producer aka big brother who pays your Wi-Fi bill.)

Laughter bounced off the walls, easy and unfiltered.

They gathered around the dinner table a non-negotiable ritual in the Kapoor household. Yes it's a rule in Sharma house that everyone should have dinner together.

Akash:
“Office kaise chal raha hai?”
(How’s work going?)

Kavitha: (sternly)
“Bas karo tum sab. Ab sirf khaana. No office talk, no game talk. Sirf family time.”
(That’s enough, all of you. Now just food. No office talk, no game talk. Only family time.)

Avinav:
“Ji, madam. Full focus on dal, chawal, aur maa ka pyaar.”
(Yes, madam. Full focus on lentils, rice, and mom’s love.)

Pranav:
“Waise aaj ka okra toh world class lag raha hai, Mom.”
(By the way, today’s okra looks world class, Mom.)

Kavitha:
“Beta, jab tak plate khali na ho, tareef valid nahi hoti.”
(Son, compliments don’t count until the plate is empty.)

The table filled with laughter again, overlapping voices, light teasing.

The clink of steel spoons, the aroma of home-cooked food, and the easy rhythm of shared routine filled the room with a warmth that no skyline view or award winning design could match.

For Avinav, this was the blueprint of happiness unpolished, loud, and absolutely perfect.

                   ******************
Next Morning 
Kapoor Residence – 6:30 AM

The sun was still stretching its rays over the skyline when soft sounds filled the Kapoor household.

In the kitchen, the gentle clinking of spoons and boiling water announced the start of the day.

Kavitha, tying her dupatta, called out:
“Kya, Akash ji? Chalna hai ya aaj bhi neend jeet gayi?”
(What, Akash ji? Are we going or did sleep win again today?)

Akash emerged from their room, stretching and yawning:
“Main toh taiyaar hoon. Aaj tum late ho, Mrs. Kapoor.”
(I’m ready. You’re the one who’s late today, Mrs. Kapoor.)

They shared a smile, slipped on their walking shoes, and headed out for their daily walk to the nearby park.

At the same time, upstairs—

Avinav, dressed in joggers and a plain black tee, nudged Pranav with his foot.
“Uth, gym partner. Warna main akela chala jaunga aur tujhe dumbbells uthane ke sapne mein chhod dunga.”
(Get up, gym partner. Or I’m leaving you to lift dumbbells in your dreams.)

Pranav groaned from under his blanket.
“Bhai… cardio is a scam.”
(Bro… cardio is a scam.)

Avinav laughed, tossing a towel at him.
“Tu scam hai. Chal.”
(You’re the scam. Let’s go.)

Within minutes, both brothers were out, earbuds in, heading to their gym a few blocks away.

Aaryan’s Residence – Same Time

The kitchen smelled of fresh toast and Ruhi’s favorite cinnamon tea.

Zoya, already dressed in light workout clothes, was scrolling through her phone when Ruhi entered.

Ruhi:
“You’re up early. Jet lag finally gave up?”
(With a teasing grin)

Zoya chuckled, sipping her tea.
“Either that, or your chai has magical powers.”

Aaryan walked in, adjusting his smartwatch.
“We’re heading to the park. Want to come along? It’s nearby. Peaceful. Good start to the day.”

Zoya thought for a second, then smiled.
“Sounds perfect.”

Ruhi:
“Let’s go then. You can meet some of our neighbors too. They’re sweet.”

Zoya grabbed her bottle.
“Lead the way.”

Park – 7:00 AM

The park was alive with soft jogs, light laughter, and scattered conversations. Old couples walked in synchrony, kids practiced yoga with their parents, and the scent of mogra flowers lingered in the air.

Akash and Kavitha took their usual loop near the central trail, waving to a few familiar faces.

Meanwhile, Aaryan, Ruhi, and Zoya entered from the other side.

Kavitha nudged Akash gently.
“Aaryan aur Ruhi toh hain… lekin ye saath mein kaun hai? Nayi lag rahi hai.”
(That’s Aaryan and Ruhi… but who’s with them? She looks new.)

Akash squinted.
“Haan… pehli baar dekh raha hoon. Shayad guest ho.”
(Yes… seeing her for the first time. Maybe a guest.)

The trio came closer, and Aaryan greeted them with a warm smile.
“Good morning, Uncle! Good morning, Aunty!”

Ruhi followed with a cheerful, “Morning!”

Aaryan (gesturing):
“Yeh meri behen hai — Zoya. Abhi UK se aayi hai. Kuch time yahin rahegi.”

Zoya folded her hands politely.
“Namaste. Bahut accha laga aap dono se milke.”
(Namaste. It’s lovely to meet you both.)

Kavitha smiled warmly.
“Namaste beta. Tumhare baare mein Aaryan aur Ruhi se suna toh tha… milne ka mauka aaj mila.”
(We’ve heard about you from Aaryan and Ruhi... nice to finally meet you.)

Akash (pleasantly):
“UK mein kya karti ho beta?”
(What do you do in the UK, dear?)

Zoya:
“Main architect hoon. Design aur restoration ka kaam karti hoon.”
(I’m an architect. I work in design and restoration.)

Kavitha’s expression softened, impressed. She exchanged a quiet, approving glance with Akash.

Kavitha:
“Wah. Bahut hi shant aur samajhdar lagti ho.”
(Wow. You seem very calm and mature.)

Zoya:
“Thank you, Aunty.”

Kavitha:
“Chalo, aaj tum log mere walking partners ban jao.”
(Alright, today you all are my walking partners.)

Ruhi and Zoya laughed softly, exchanging amused glances as they walked ahead with Kavitha.

Ahead on the Path – Kavitha, Zoya & Ruhi
Kavitha walked steadily between the two girls, occasionally glancing at Zoya with growing fondness. After a comfortable pause in their light chatter about the park’s greenery, she finally spoke up, her tone gentle and curious.

Kavitha (smiling):
“Zoya beta, ek personal sawaal pooch sakti hoon? Bura toh nahi manogi?”
(Zoya dear, may I ask you a personal question? Hope you won’t mind?)

Zoya (with a respectful nod):
“Nahi Aunty, bilkul nahi. Poochiye.”
(Not at all, Aunty. Please ask.)

Kavitha (half-playful, half-curious):
“Shaadi ke baare mein kab soch rahi ho? Ya abhi sirf office aur projects hi priority hain?”
(When are you planning to think about marriage? Or is it all work and projects for now?)

Zoya blinked, a little surprised but not flustered. She responded with calm grace.
Zoya:
“Filhaal toh career hi chal raha hai Aunty… kabhi kabhi lagta hai din 24 nahi 14 ghante ke hi hain. Shaadi ka thought parked hai — future lane mein.”
(For now it’s mostly career, Aunty… sometimes feels like the day has just 14 hours instead of 24. Marriage thoughts are parked — in the future lane.)

Ruhi chuckled and nudged Zoya lightly.
Ruhi (teasing):
“Aunty, iska calendar full booked hota hai. Shaadi ke liye koi Sunday bhi free nahi milti.”
(Aunty, her calendar’s always full. Can’t even find a free Sunday for marriage.)

Zoya laughed, mock rolling her eyes.
Zoya:
“Ruhi bas kar yaar! Aunty sach mein sochengi main career ke alawa kuch sochti hi nahi.”
(Ruhi, stop it! Aunty will really think I don’t consider anything beyond work.)

Kavitha (laughing with them, then softening):
“Arey nahi beta… aisa kuch nahi. Tumhari tarah ki ladkiyon ke liye toh log dua karte hain. Samjhdari, tehzeeb, aur ek shant smile — ye qualities rare ho gayi hain.”
(Oh no dear… nothing like that. Girls like you are what people pray for — maturity, grace, and that calm smile — such qualities have become rare.)

Zoya’s expression softened. She looked genuinely touched.
Zoya:
“Aapka shukriya Aunty… aap jaise log mil jayein toh life thoda easy lagta hai.”
(Thank you, Aunty… meeting people like you makes life feel a bit easier.)

Kavitha gently tapped her arm.
Kavitha:
“Beta, jab niyat saaf ho… toh raahein khud seedhi ho jaati hain.”
(When intentions are pure, paths align on their own.)

Ruhi, walking beside them, smirked slightly.
Ruhi:
“Aur jab Kavitha Aunty compliments dene lagein… samajh jao seedhi raahein kuchh aur bhi plan kar rahi hain.”
(And when Kavitha Aunty starts giving compliments… know that those straight paths might be planning something more too.)

Zoya chuckled nervously while Kavitha simply smiled with that twinkle in her eyes — no denial, just a quiet hint.


A Few Steps Behind – Akash and Aaryan
Akash, walking in rhythm with Aaryan, spoke quietly, choosing his words with care.
Akash:
“Aapki behen… Zoya… bahut achhi ladki hai. Sukoon hai uske lehze mein, sanskaar dikhta hai uske tareeke mein.”
(Your sister… Zoya… she’s a lovely girl. There’s peace in her voice, and her manner shows values.)

Aaryan looked mildly surprised but appreciated the compliment.
Aaryan:
“Shukriya Uncle. Haan, woh waise hi hai. Thodi reserved, lekin dil ki bahut clean hai.”
(Thank you, Uncle. Yes, she’s like that—bit reserved, but very clean-hearted.)

Akash nodded, eyes still observing the women ahead.
Akash (gently, yet directly):
“Dekhiye, hum zyada nahi jaante aap logon ko… lekin jitna samjha hai, lagta hai aap log izzatdaar aur samajhdar log ho. Agar kabhi aap log kisi rishte ke baare mein soch rahe ho… toh mujhe lagta hai mera beta Avinav ek achha match ho sakta hai.”
(Look, we don’t know each other very well… but from what I’ve seen, you all seem like honorable and wise people. If you ever happen to think about a match… I believe my son Avinav might be a good match.)

Aaryan took a moment, thoughtful. The tone was respectful—no pressure, just a quiet suggestion.
Aaryan (politely):
“Main samjha Uncle. Main baat ghar pe zaroor karunga.”
(I understand, Uncle. I’ll surely discuss it at home.)

Akash smiled faintly, not pressing further. The matter was left in the air—soft, open-ended, and full of quiet possibilities.

Akash and Aaryan fell into step a few paces behind them, their pace a bit slower.

Sharma Residence – Late Morning
The front door clicked open as Kavitha and Akash stepped in after their morning walk. Kavitha headed straight to the kitchen to start the chai while Akash dropped his cap on the side table and stretched his shoulders.

Moments later, the gate opened again. Avinav and Pranav returned from the gym, both in their tracksuits, towels around their necks.

Pranav (exhausted):
“Chest day should be illegal.”

Avinav (grinning slightly):
“Especially for people who skip leg day.”

Kavitha (from the kitchen):
“Chai ban rahi hai. Aate hi fridge mein mat ghus jaana dono!”
(Tea’s on the stove. Don’t jump into the fridge the moment you come in!)

Akash (settling onto the sofa):
“Aaj thoda thand zyada lag rahi hai. Dhoop bhi kam thi.”
(It felt a little chilly today. Sun wasn’t out much either.)

Avinav (heading toward the stairs):
“I’ll freshen up.”

Pranav (following):
“Mujhe towel chahiye... tumhara le lu kya?”
(I need a towel... can I take yours?)

Avinav (without turning):
“Nahi.”
(No.)

Kavitha smiled faintly as she poured the chai into cups. The house filled with the familiar, comforting hum of routine.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Chapter - 4 

The soft footsteps of Avinav and Pranav faded upstairs, leaving a calm hush in the living room. 

Kavitha poured steaming chai into two cups, her eyes thoughtful as she glanced at Akash, who was flipping through the newspaper with an unusually distracted air.

Kavitha: (softly)
“Zoya… kitni achchi ladki hai na?” (Zoya… what a nice girl, isn’t she?)

Akash lowered the paper slightly, a small smile tugging at his lips.

Akash:
“Haan. Samajhdar, confident… aur uska professionalism bhi dikh raha tha.” (Yes. Mature, confident… and her professionalism was evident too.)

Kavitha set the teapot down, her voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper.

Kavitha:
“Pata hai, mujhe laga ki—”

Before she could finish, Akash chuckled, folding the newspaper.

Akash:
“Tum rishta bhejne ki soch rahi ho, na? Main park mein hi Aaryan se baat kar chuka hoon.” (You’re thinking of sending a proposal, aren’t you? I already spoke to Aaryan at the park.)

Kavitha’s eyes widened.

Kavitha: (mock-scolding)
“Akash ji! Aapne mujhse puchne ka bhi intezaar nahi kiya?” (You didn’t even wait to ask me?)

Akash: (grinning)
“Tumhare expressions dekh ke hi pata chal gaya tha tum kya soch rahi thi.” (Your face told me exactly what you were thinking.)

Kavitha huffed but couldn’t hide her smile.

Kavitha:
“Toh? Kya kaha Aaryan ne?” (So? What did Aaryan say?)

Akash: (leaning back)
“Usne kaha ki woh ghar mein discuss karega. Lekin… unhone mana nahi kiya.” (He said he’d discuss it at home. But… they didn’t refuse.)

A slow, satisfied smile spread across Kavitha’s face.

Akash:
"Beta, park mein ek joda mila—Aaryan aur Ruhi. Unhe hum kaafi samay se jaante hain. Bahut hi acche log hain."
(Son, we met a couple in the park—Aaryan and Ruhi. We've known them for a long time. They're really good people.)
"Dono ke maa-baap ab nahi rahe, lekin dono ne mehnat karke acchi padaai ki, phir ek doosre se mile aur prem vivaah kiya."
(Both of them lost their parents, but they worked hard, studied well, then met each other and had a love marriage.)
"Aaryan ne apni behen ko padhai ke liye UK bheja tha."
(Aaryan even sent his sister to the UK for education.)

Avinav:
"Wow, itna inspiring hai. Is duniya mein itne selfless log milte kahan hain. Lekin... aap yeh sab abhi kyun keh rahe ho, papa?"
(Wow, that’s really inspiring. In today’s selfish world, such people are rare. But… why are you suddenly telling me all this, papa?)

Akash (gently):
"Aaj hum uski behen se mile... kaam ke silsile mein yahan aayi hai UK se. Main aur tumhari maa ko woh ladki achhi lagi... tumhare liye."
(Today we met his sister... she’s come here for work from the UK. Your mother and I liked her… for you.)

Avinav (a bit caught off-guard):
"Papa... not today, please. Itna achanak? I don’t think I’m ready for marriage."
(Papa… not today, please. So suddenly? I don’t think I’m ready for marriage.)

Pranav (with a cheeky grin):
"Haan papa, bhai toh seedha retirement ke baad shaadi karega. Abhi toh bas kaam, kaam aur kaam!"
(Yeah papa, bhai will get married straight after retirement. Right now, it’s just work, work, and more work!)

Avinav (rolling his eyes):
"Chup kar yaar! Papa, aapne kuch kaha toh nahi na Aaryan ko?"
(Shut up, man! Papa, I hope you didn’t say anything to Aaryan?)

Akash (half-smiling):
"Beta... baat nikal gayi thi. Maine casually bataya ki humein woh ladki pasand aayi. Usne kaha woh apne family se baat karega."
(Son… it came up. I casually mentioned we liked the girl. He said he’ll talk to his family.)
"As far as I know, response positive hi hoga."
(As far as I know, the response will be positive.)

Kavitha (gently joining in):
"Aur beta, woh bhi architect hai. Naam hai—Zoya."
(And son, she’s also an architect. Her name is—Zoya.)

At the mention of the name "Zoya", Avinav pauses. His mind flashes to the confident voice and subtle charm of the woman he briefly interacted with over video call. Then he brushes it off.

Avinav (to himself, half aloud):
"Zoya... Hmm. UK se padhi hai, architect hai... itne coincidence ho sakte hain. Shayad koi aur hogi."
(Zoya… Studied in the UK, architect… could just be coincidence. Probably someone else.)

Still, a small part of him wonders… what if it’s the same girl?

Pranav (leaning in dramatically):
"Bhaaiiiii... lagta hai bhai already future wife ke baare mein soch raha hai!"
(Broooo… looks like big bro is already thinking about his future wife!)

Avinav (sighs):
"Bas karo yaar, majak nahi hai."
(Stop it, man. This isn’t a joke.)

Akash (reassuring):
"Beta, yeh compulsion nahi hai. Agar tum abhi ready nahi ho toh main Aaryan ko bata dunga."
(Son, there’s no pressure. If you’re not ready, I’ll let Aaryan know.)

Avinav (after thinking for a moment):
"Nahi papa... aisa nahi hai. Soch ke bola toh... theek hai. Agar unki taraf se haan aayi toh weekend pe mil lete hain."
(No papa… it’s not like that. After thinking about it… okay. If they say yes, we’ll visit them this weekend.)
"Shaadi ke liye haan tabhi kehunga jab mujhe ladki pasand aaye."
(I’ll only say yes for marriage if I genuinely like the girl.)

Pranav (grinning ear to ear):
"Lo ji! Bhaai ki taraf se haan aa gayi!"
(There it is! A yes from big bro!)
"Ab toh office mein bhi kaam, ghar pe bhi kaam. Bhai ki life full architect mode!"
(Now it’ll be architecture at the office and at home. Bro’s life is in full architect mode!)

Avinav (chuckling):
"Main itna boring nahi hoon, overacting band kar."
(I’m not that boring. Stop overacting.)

Kavitha (gently):
"Sure ho na beta? Hum haan bol dein Aaryan ko?"
(You’re sure, son? Should we say yes to Aaryan?)

Avinav:
"Haan maa, aap dono ko agar ladki pasand aayi toh woh achhi hi hogi."
(Yes mom, if you and papa liked her, I’m sure she must be good.)
"Main office ke liye nikalta hoon, der ho rahi hai."
(I’m leaving for office now, getting late.)

Akash:
"Beta, Ehsen ko bhi bata dena. Weekend mein milne ka ek reason mil gaya. Kaafi din ho gaye usse mile."
(Son, inform Ehsen too. This gives us a reason to meet him on the weekend. It’s been a while.)

Pranav:
"Haan bhai, teesra beta bhi toh apni hone waali bhabhi se milne ka haq rakhta hai!"
(Yeah bhai, the third son also deserves to meet his future bhabhi!)

Avinav (half-smiling while getting up):
"Theek hai papa."
(Alright papa.)
